styles.name: Visual Learner
styles.description: Learns best through images, diagrams, charts, and spatial understanding. Thinks in pictures and can easily visualize concepts.
characteristics: Prefers diagrams and flowcharts over text explanations
Uses color coding and highlighting in notes
Draws mind maps and concept maps
Remembers faces better than names
Struggles with long lectures without visual aids
Benefits from whiteboard explanations

studyTechniques.name: Diagramming
studyTechniques.description: Draw concept maps, flowcharts, and diagrams to represent relationships between ideas

studyTechniques.name: Color Coding
studyTechniques.description: Use different colors for categories, importance levels, or types of information in notes

studyTechniques.name: Visual Flashcards
studyTechniques.description: Flashcards that include images, diagrams, or color cues alongside text

studyTechniques.name: Video Learning
studyTechniques.description: Watch educational videos with animations and visual demonstrations

studyTechniques.name: Sketchnoting
studyTechniques.description: Take notes with a mix of drawings, symbols, and minimal text

teachingAdaptations: Use analogies that paint a picture ('imagine a factory where...')
Include ASCII diagrams and visual representations in explanations
Describe spatial relationships explicitly
Suggest drawing the concept as part of learning
Use tables and structured visual layouts for information

styles.name: Auditory Learner
styles.description: Learns best through listening and speaking. Processes information through sound, rhythm, and verbal explanation.
characteristics: Prefers lectures and verbal explanations
Talks through problems out loud
Remembers things said to them better than things read
Benefits from discussion and group study
Uses rhythm and music as memory aids
Can follow oral instructions well

studyTechniques.name: Verbal Repetition
studyTechniques.description: Explain concepts out loud in your own words, as if teaching someone

studyTechniques.name: Discussion Groups
studyTechniques.description: Study with others and discuss topics, debate ideas, and quiz each other verbally

studyTechniques.name: Audio Recording
studyTechniques.description: Record yourself explaining key concepts and listen back during commutes or downtime

studyTechniques.name: Mnemonics and Rhymes
studyTechniques.description: Create songs, rhymes, or rhythmic patterns to encode information

studyTechniques.name: Read Aloud
studyTechniques.description: Read textbook material and notes out loud rather than silently

teachingAdaptations: Use clear, conversational explanations with verbal cues
Suggest saying concepts out loud as a study strategy
Use alliteration, rhyme, and rhythm in mnemonics
Encourage verbal self-quizzing
Present information in a narrative, storytelling format

styles.name: Kinesthetic Learner
styles.description: Learns best through hands-on experience, physical activity, and doing. Needs to engage physically with material to truly understand it.
characteristics: Prefers labs, experiments, and hands-on projects
Fidgets during long lectures but concentrates well during activities
Learns by doing rather than watching or listening
Remembers experiences and physical activities
Benefits from taking frequent breaks and moving around
Good at understanding how things work through manipulation

studyTechniques.name: Hands-On Practice
studyTechniques.description: Work through problems physically, build models, conduct experiments

studyTechniques.name: Role Playing
studyTechniques.description: Act out historical events, scientific processes, or conceptual scenarios

studyTechniques.name: Movement-Based Review
studyTechniques.description: Walk around while reviewing flashcards, or associate topics with physical locations (memory palace)

studyTechniques.name: Teaching with Objects
studyTechniques.description: Use physical objects to represent abstract concepts (coins for math, household items for science)

studyTechniques.name: Break-Based Learning
studyTechniques.description: Use short, active study blocks with physical breaks in between

teachingAdaptations: Use physical analogies ('imagine you are the electron...')
Suggest building or drawing something physical
Break explanations into active steps the student can do
Recommend walking while reviewing or reciting
Use gesture-based memory cues and body-based mnemonics

styles.name: Reading/Writing Learner
styles.description: Learns best through text-based input and output. Excels at reading textbooks, taking detailed notes, and expressing understanding through writing.
characteristics: Prefers textbooks and written materials over lectures
Takes detailed, organized notes
Writes summaries and rewrites notes to study
Good at expressing ideas in writing
Comfortable with dense, text-heavy resources
Benefits from making lists and outlines

studyTechniques.name: Cornell Notes
studyTechniques.description: Structured note-taking with main ideas, details, and summary sections

studyTechniques.name: Rewriting Notes
studyTechniques.description: Rewrite and reorganize notes in your own words after class

studyTechniques.name: Practice Essays
studyTechniques.description: Write out full explanations and essays for key concepts

studyTechniques.name: Text Annotation
studyTechniques.description: Highlight, underline, and annotate textbook readings with marginal notes

studyTechniques.name: List Making
studyTechniques.description: Convert concepts into organized lists, outlines, and bullet-point summaries

teachingAdaptations: Provide written explanations with clear structure
Suggest writing summaries after each explanation
Use numbered steps and organized bullet points
Recommend specific textbook sections for deeper reading
Encourage journaling about what was learned

multiModal.description: Most people are not purely one style. Effective learning often combines multiple modalities. The key is identifying which modality is strongest and supplementing with others.

recommendedCombos.strategy: Draw diagrams and then physically trace or recreate them. Use hands-on models alongside visual aids.
Read notes aloud. Record written summaries and listen back. Discuss written material in study groups.
Use color-coded notes with diagrams. Create visual outlines before writing. Annotate diagrams with text.
Build physical models and sketch them. Walk while reviewing visual flashcards. Use gesture with diagrams.

assessmentQuestions: When learning something new, do you prefer to see a diagram, hear an explanation, try it yourself, or read about it?
How do you usually remember directions — from a map, from someone telling you, from walking the route, or from written steps?
When studying, what helps most — watching a video, discussing with a friend, doing practice problems, or reading your notes?
In class, do you learn best from slides, lectures, labs, or textbook readings?
When you get stuck on a problem, do you sketch it out, talk it through, try different approaches, or look up the theory?
